figures.caption: FIG 1. Time series plot (left) and Hill plot (right) of the measles dataset.
FIG 5. One-step ahead predicted values of the measles data count time series (in red) and true value of the time series (in blue). The testing set size is ntest = 246.

FIG 8. Heatmaps of the real part of the spectrum for the causal integer-valued AR(1) model in Example 6, with parameters δ = 2, p= 0.3, α= 0.7, and λ values equally spaced over [0,2π] with nine points.

FIG 9. Heatmaps of the imaginary part of the spectrum for the causal integer-valued AR(1) model in Example 6, with parameters δ = 2, p= 0.3, α= 0.7, and λ values equally spaced over [0,2π] with nine points.

FIG 2. Heatmaps of the real part (left panel) and the imaginary part (right panel) of the spectrum for the causal integer-valued AR(1) model in Example 6, with parameters δ = 2, p= 0.3, α= 0.7, and λ= 0.785.
FIG 6. Heatmaps of the real part of the spectrum for the causal INMA(1) model in Example 5, with parameters δ = 2, p= 0.3, α= 0.7, and λ values equally spaced over [0,2π] with nine points.
FIG 7. Heatmaps of the imaginary part of the spectrum for the causal INMA(1) model in Example 5, with parameters δ = 2, p= 0.3, α= 0.7, and λ values equally spaced over [0,2π] with nine points.

FIG 3. Histograms of ân and δ̂n (in green) with respect to limiting Gaussian distribution (in red) in the noncausal AR(1) model with Cauchy innovations. 2000 replication, n= 500 and (a, δ) = (1.3,2).
FIG 11. Histograms of ân and δ̂n (in green) with respect to limiting Gaussian distribution (in red) in the causal AR(1) model with Cauchy innovations. 2000 replications, n= 500 and (a, δ) = (0.7,2).
FIG 10. Histograms of â−1 n and δ̂n (in green) with respect to limiting Gaussian distribution (in red) in the MA(1) model with Cauchy innovations. 2000 replications, n= 500 and (a, δ) = (0.8−1,1).

FIG 12. Histograms of p̂n and δ̂n (in green) with respect to limiting Gaussian distribution (in red) in the INMA(1) model with discrete stable innovations. 2000 replication, n= 500 and (p, δ) = (0.3,2). The rate of selection of the proper α= 0.7 is a hundred percent.

FIG 4. Histograms of p̂n and δ̂n (in green) with respect to limiting Gaussian distribution (in red) in the INAR(1) model with discrete stable innovations. 2000 replication, n= 500 and (p, δ) = (0.3,2). The rate of selection of the proper α= 0.7 is a hundred percent.
